serving.natural_language_portion: One standard appetizer serving of Babycorn Manchurian is approximately 250 g, typically served as a starter for one person in an Indo-Chinese restaurant setting.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Baby corn, raw (FDC ID 168393) and composite estimation for fried vegetable dish with Manchurian sauce
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Babycorn Manchurian exists in USDA FoodData Central, HPB Singapore Foodo, or ICMR-NIN databases. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for baby corn (raw), combined with standard deep-frying oil absorption factors and typical Manchurian sauce ingredient contributions (soy sauce, ketchup, cornstarch batter). Input record values (energy 180 kcal, protein 3.2 g, fat 12.0 g, carbohydrate 16.0 g, sodium 480 mg per 100 g) are consistent with this composite estimation and have been retained with confidence level 'medium'. Cholesterol is 0.0 mg as this is a fully plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients; this is confirmed zero by formulation.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — Analogous estimate based on deep-fried battered vegetables and refined starch coating
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Babycorn Manchurian. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on analogous deep-fried battered vegetable dishes with refined starch coatings (cornstarch and all-purpose flour batter, GI ~65–70) from the University of Sydney GI Database. The Manchurian sauce contributes added sugar (4 g/100 g) and ketchup, which slightly elevates the glycaemic response. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (65) × available carbohydrate per serving (40 g from 250 g portion) / 100 = 26.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 480 mg per 100 g; a 250 g serving delivers 1200 mg sodium, representing approximately 52% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2300 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Babycorn Manchurian to very small portions or avoid it entirely; request preparation with reduced soy sauce and low-sodium alternatives, and avoid adding extra salt.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Estimated GI of 65 (medium-high) combined with 16 g carbohydrate per 100 g and 4 g added sugar per 100 g from Manchurian sauce; a 250 g serving delivers approximately 40 g available carbohydrate with a glycemic load of 26, which is high.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume Babycorn Manchurian in very small portions only, request air-fried or baked preparation to reduce fat and GI impact, and ask for reduced ketchup and chilli sauce to lower added sugar content.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat 12 g per 100 g with saturated fat 2 g per 100 g and trans fat 0.2 g per 100 g from deep-frying; sodium at 480 mg per 100 g further elevates cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit intake due to high total fat from deep-frying, the presence of trans fat, and high sodium; choosing a stir-fried or baked preparation with reduced sauce significantly lowers the cardiovascular risk profile.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end products (AGEs) that promote joint inflammation; capsicum and green chillies are nightshade ingredients that may exacerbate symptoms in sensitive individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried preparations of Babycorn Manchurian, omit nightshade ingredients (capsicum, green chillies) if sensitive, and request a stir-fried or baked version with reduced soy sauce.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Babycorn Manchurian is a popular Indo-Chinese appetizer widely served in Indian restaurants, food courts, and hawker-style outlets across Singapore and India. It consists of tender baby corn cobs coated in a seasoned batter of all-purpose flour and cornstarch, deep-fried until golden and crispy, then tossed in a bold Manchurian sauce made from soy sauce, tomato ketchup, chilli sauce, garlic, ginger, spring onion, and capsicum. The dish delivers a satisfying contrast of textures — a crunchy exterior giving way to the naturally sweet, slightly fibrous baby corn inside — with a flavour profile that is simultaneously spicy, tangy, savoury, and mildly sweet. It is fully vegetarian and vegan, containing no animal-derived ingredients, and is typically served as a dry starter or with a light gravy. Nutritionally, a 100 g portion provides approximately 180 kcal, 3.2 g protein, 12 g fat (including 2 g saturated fat and 0.2 g trans fat from deep-frying), and 16 g carbohydrate with 4 g added sugar. Sodium is high at 480 mg per 100 g, driven by soy sauce and chilli sauce, making this dish a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The estimated glycemic index of 65 and a glycemic load of 26 per standard 250 g serving make it a high-GL food unsuitable for frequent consumption by people with Type 2 Diabetes. The deep-fried preparation also generates advanced glycation end products (AGEs), which are relevant for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis. Healthier modifications include air-frying or baking the battered baby corn and reducing the sauce volume to lower sodium and sugar intake.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Babycorn Manchurian exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, USDA FoodData Central). Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central baby corn data combined with standard deep-frying absorption factors and Manchurian sauce ingredient contributions. The input record values are consistent with this composite estimation and have been retained. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database based on comparable deep-fried battered vegetable dishes.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite deep-fried dish is not available in USDA FoodData Central or the HPB Singapore database as of 2026-04-16. 2. potassium_mg is set to -1: potassium data for this specific composite dish is not reported in available approved sources; estimated ~180 mg per 100 g based on baby corn composition but not confirmed by an approved source and therefore set to -1. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this composite Indo-Chinese fried dish are not available in USDA FoodData Central or HPB Singapore Foodo as of 2026-04-16. 4. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero by formulation — Babycorn Manchurian is a fully plant-based dish containing no animal-derived ingredients; cholesterol is naturally zero. 5. trans_fat_g at 0.2 g per 100 g is retained from the input record as consistent with small amounts of trans fat generated during commercial deep-frying of vegetable oil. 6. The input record description contained an erroneous reference to 'Butter Chicken dish'; this has been disregarded as Babycorn Manchurian is a vegetarian dish with no chicken content. 7. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate; direct GI measurement for Babycorn Manchurian has not been published in peer-reviewed literature as of 2026-04-16.
